question: Based on Table 5.2-5 chemistry limits and the CVCS text description, does the NuScale chemistry program adequately prevent corrosion degradation?

nuscale_ch05_0007.title: Integrity of Reactor Coolant Boundary
nuscale_ch05_0007.summary: This section describes the design features of each NuScale Power Module (NPM) that ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) throughout the plant’s design life. It details the components and systems included in the RCPB, such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment isolation valves, safety valves, and associated piping, all of which are designed to meet regulatory requirements and maintain pressure containment.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment with current instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is determined, and leakage rates are monitored through RCS inventory balance and comparison with the containment evacuation system (CES) flow. The leakage detection systems are designed to be reliable, redundant, and sensitive, supporting leak-before-break safety analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) code cases, as mandated by various sections of 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC-endorsed standards, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) comply with relevant regulatory requirements, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria, by designing, fabricating, and testing reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Class 1 standards. It also clarifies the classification of connected piping and references the use of the 2013 Edition of the ASME BPVC as the code of record for the design certification.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, relying on integrated components within each NuScale Power Module (NPM). The RCS is designed to safely transfer heat from the reactor core, maintain proper coolant chemistry, and ensure uniform boron concentration, supporting both normal and off-normal plant conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0010.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Materials
nuscale_ch05_0010.summary: The Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) materials, including welds, are selected and fabricated in accordance with ASME BPVC standards and comply with relevant U.S. regulatory requirements to ensure high quality, environmental compatibility, and fracture resistance. These materials are subject to strict quality assurance measures and testing to meet fracture toughness criteria, with detailed specifications provided in Table 5.2-4.

nuscale_ch05_0021.title: Reactor Coolant System Piping
nuscale_ch05_0021.summary: The section discusses the design and degradation assessment of the NuScale reactor coolant system's steam generator (SG) tubing. It notes that the main degradation observed in similar operating reactors is wear from flow-induced vibration or foreign objects, and that NuScale's design—with its helical tubing and novel support structure—does not increase risk compared to existing designs. Prototypic testing and standard SG program guidelines are deemed sufficient, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism and secondary corrosion risks considered low.

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: The steam generators (SGs) in the NuScale Power Module are integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which also houses other key components such as the pressurizer and safety valves. The SG system includes feedwater and main steam piping, thermal relief valves, and access ports, with the RPV itself forming the outer pressure boundary for the SGs and contributing to the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report regarding the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary. It includes guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as specific ASME code cases relevant to reactor component materials and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer, integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and separated by a baffle plate, regulates reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, with instrumentation providing real-time pressure and level data to control systems.

edges.description: Section A outlines the requirement to maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), while Section B describes the methods and limitations of detecting leakage from the RCPB, both of which are connected by their focus on ensuring RCPB integrity as a prerequisite for preventing corrosion degradation.

Section A describes the design features and regulatory definitions ensuring the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B details compliance with ASME and NRC code requirements for overpressure protection, both of which are necessary prerequisites for evaluating whether the chemistry program (from Table 5.2-5 and CVCS text) adequately prevents corrosion degradation.

Section A describes the design objective to maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B specifies that this integrity is achieved by complying with regulatory codes and standards such as 10 CFR 50.55a and ASME BPVC.

Section B provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's components and function, which Section A then specifies in detail regarding the integrity requirements and regulatory definitions for those same components.

Section B specifies the material standards and regulatory requirements that the RCPB must meet, which are referenced in Section A's discussion of maintaining RCPB integrity over the plant's design life.

Section A discusses the challenges in detecting reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage, while Section B outlines compliance with ASME and NRC overpressure protection requirements, connecting them through their shared focus on maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary as required by regulatory codes.

Section A describes the practical limitations of leakage detection in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B states that the design and construction comply with regulatory codes (10 CFR 50.55a and ASME BPVC), connecting them through the regulatory framework that underpins both leakage detection requirements and design standards.

Section B provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system design, which Section A references in its discussion of leakage detection capabilities and limitations, specifically noting the absence of traditional components described in Section B.

Section B specifies that the RCPB materials comply with ASME and regulatory requirements, which is a prerequisite for the leakage detection considerations discussed in Section A, as material integrity affects leakage potential.

Section A references the use of ASME BPVC code cases for overpressure protection, while Section B specifies that all relevant reactor coolant pressure boundary components comply with 10 CFR 50.55a and ASME BPVC requirements, thus Section A provides details for the general compliance statement in Section B.

Section A references the applicable ASME BPVC code cases and regulatory provisions for overpressure protection, while Section B specifies that the reactor coolant pressure boundary materials conform to those same ASME BPVC requirements, connecting them through shared compliance with regulatory and code standards.

Section A states that the reactor coolant pressure boundary components comply with ASME Code requirements, while Section B provides a general description of the reactor coolant system's design and components, with Section A's compliance applying specifically to the systems described in Section B.

Section A states that the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components comply with regulatory and ASME BPVC requirements, while Section B specifies that the materials used for the RCPB also conform to the same ASME BPVC standards and regulations, thus Section B provides the material-specific details that support Section A's general compliance claim.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's design and function, while Section B specifies the regulatory and material standards that the reactor coolant pressure boundary materials must meet, connecting them through the requirement that the system's materials and design must collectively ensure integrity and safety.

Section A discusses the observed degradation mechanisms in reactor coolant system piping, while Section B outlines the requirement to maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, connecting them through the need to prevent degradation (such as corrosion) to satisfy regulatory integrity requirements.

Section A discusses the observed degradation mechanisms (primarily wear, not corrosion) in reactor coolant system piping, while Section B addresses the detection of pressure boundary leakage, both of which are relevant to evaluating whether the chemistry program (referenced in Table 5.2-5) is effective in preventing corrosion-related degradation and ensuring system integrity.

Section A discusses observed degradation mechanisms in reactor coolant system piping, while Section B outlines compliance with ASME and NRC regulatory codes for system integrity, connecting them through the shared concern of maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary against degradation.
Section A describes the components of the reactor coolant system, including steam generators, which are part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary whose integrity is the focus of Section B.

Section A describes the components of the reactor coolant system, including steam generators, while Section B discusses the detection of leakage from the reactor coolant pressure boundary, both of which are relevant to assessing whether chemistry limits and control (from Table 5.2-5 and CVCS) are sufficient to prevent corrosion that could lead to leakage.

Section A discusses observed degradation mechanisms in steam generator tubing, specifically noting that corrosion is not a significant issue due to material selection and operational experience, while Section B provides a general description of the steam generators' design and components, establishing the context for Section A's detailed discussion.

Section A lists references, including industry chemistry guidelines, that are used to support the claims in Section B regarding the design features maintaining reactor coolant boundary integrity, specifically by referencing standards relevant to corrosion prevention.

Section A provides references to industry chemistry guidelines and standards that inform the corrosion prevention approach, while Section B describes the system's leakage detection limitations, both of which are connected by their relevance to ensuring the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary against corrosion degradation.

Section A lists references to industry chemistry guidelines and materials reliability programs that inform the corrosion prevention approach, while Section B describes compliance with ASME codes and regulatory requirements for reactor coolant boundary integrity, connecting them through the shared objective of ensuring system integrity and regulatory compliance.

Section A describes the pressurizer as a component of the reactor vessel that is part of the reactor coolant system, while Section B defines the integrity requirements for the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which includes the pressurizer as a pressure-retaining component; thus, Section A provides specific details about a component that is generally described in Section B.

Section A describes the design and function of the pressurizer as part of the reactor coolant system, while Section B discusses the detection of leakage in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, both of which are connected by their roles in maintaining the integrity and operational safety of the reactor coolant system, which is relevant to assessing the effectiveness of the chemistry program in preventing corrosion degradation.

Section A describes the pressurizer's design and function within the reactor coolant system, while Section B outlines the regulatory compliance requirements for reactor coolant boundary integrity, connecting them through the need for the pressurizer to meet these regulatory and code requirements.
Section A lists references, including EPRI chemistry guidelines, which provide the regulatory and technical basis for the chemistry limits described in Section B's pressurizer design text.
